{"step_by_step_thinking": "Step 1: Evaluate option A - 'Commonly presents in the 20-40 year age group'. Multiple documents (e.g., MEDCORP sources [9], [11], [12], [28]) confirm that giant cell tumors (GCT) predominantly affect young adults aged 20 to 40 years. This statement is true.

Step 2: Evaluate option B - 'Matrix consists of proliferating mononuclear cells'. Document [9] states that under the microscope, the tumor reveals three distinct cell types: mononuclear histiocytic cells, stromal cells (which produce collagen and are proliferative), and multinucleated giant cells. The mononuclear cells are described as precursors to giant cells and are part of the proliferative component. The matrix is composed of proliferating mononuclear cells (Type I and II), which are active in tumor growth. This statement is true.

Step 3: Evaluate option C - 'Osteoclast giant cells constitute the proliferative component of the tumor'. This is false. According to Document [26], the proliferative component is the mononuclear stromal cells (Type I), which produce collagen and exhibit proliferative capabilities. The multinucleated giant cells (Type III) are derived from mononuclear precursors and are not the proliferative component. They are osteoclast-like and involved in bone resorption, not proliferation. Therefore, this statement is incorrect.

Step 4: Evaluate option D - 'It is a benign tumor which may have lung metastasis'. Multiple sources (e.g., [4], [6], [8], [10], [17]) confirm that GCT is a benign but locally aggressive tumor with a potential for lung metastasis (1% to 6% of cases). This statement is true.

Conclusion: Option C is false because osteoclast giant cells are not the proliferative component; the proliferative component is the mononuclear stromal cells. Thus, the statement that 'osteoclast giant cells constitute the proliferative component' is incorrect.", "answer_choice": "C"}
